239659.explanation: We have confidence in our residents and believe in grassroots participation. From this perspective, we also believe that broadly supported initiatives contributing to the community should receive financial support. Recently, we started the villages budget as a pilot for this purpose.

239661.explanation: We must not only listen to young people but also give them actual influence over decisions that affect their future. Besides a youth council, other instruments can be used to involve young people. Tienskip is a great example of this.

239662.explanation: Everyone participates. The PvdA/GroenLinks is committed to inclusivity and will continue to be. This concerns not only LGBTIQ+ people but also accessible facilities for people with disabilities, for example.

239663.explanation: Tourists are warmly welcome in our beautiful municipality. As far as we are concerned, the current rate is sufficient.

239664.explanation: We believe there should be enough space for entrepreneurs and businesses so they can establish themselves or further develop. Because of the quality of our landscape, we prefer this to be concentrated near the larger centers.

239666.explanation: Regarding the energy transition, our municipality has a task to fulfill. Local initiatives can help, and sometimes it is good for the municipality to participate. The appropriate action must be determined case by case.

239667.explanation: Cutting costs is not an end in itself. However, a discussion about tasks could show that some municipal duties can be outsourced.

239669.explanation: We cannot prohibit companies from expanding. However, we must always strive to reduce nuisance and decrease emissions of harmful substances and odors.

239670.explanation: The Skulenboarch solar park, in which the municipality will invest, is a special, one-time opportunity. However, a broader discussion must still take place on this subject.

239671.explanation: It is very difficult for first-time buyers to enter the housing market. Therefore, it is important to support them as much as possible. This might be possible with a starter loan, as is done in some other municipalities.

239672.explanation: The municipality must adhere to national rules. Status holders are given priority for 5 to 7% of social rental housing. Because they are new to the Netherlands and could not register earlier, without this priority they would have almost no chance of obtaining housing.

239673.explanation: Integration also involves language, meeting others, development, and full participation in our community. By promoting integration, we give newcomers better opportunities.

239674.explanation: Fortunately, this already happens a lot. The municipality can stimulate this, with attention to the burden on our volunteers.

239676.explanation: With the "right to challenge," residents can always make proposals for the maintenance of trees and plants.

239677.explanation: We do not advocate mowing more often. The current approach promotes biodiversity. However, we believe it should be mowed more neatly than it is now.

239678.explanation: This fits with the inclusive idea. Everyone participates!

239682.explanation: Our (sports) clubs are struggling in these times. They therefore deserve maximum support. As a municipality, we offer multiple subsidy options.

239684.explanation: The PvdA/GroenLinks supports cooperative schools where there is room for all beliefs and where educational quality is central.

239685.explanation: This is not a task for the municipality but belongs to the national government. However, we support other initiatives at the municipal level that promote sports.

239686.explanation: This is not a task for the municipality. Tackling low literacy is a higher priority for us.

239687.explanation: We need to raise more awareness about undermining crime, especially among our youth. Municipal enforcement officers can play an important role in this.

239688.explanation: In the past, it has proven effective to hold regular consultation hours.

239692.explanation: The municipality should focus on good contact between the youth worker and the youth chain. This is not about controls, but about prevention and having meaningful conversations.

239693.explanation: Social rental housing is not allocated by the municipality but by the housing corporation. The municipality has no influence on this. The Labour Party/Green Left believes that everyone in the target group has an equal right to social rental housing.

239694.explanation: The number of social rental homes in our municipality is too low. The demand is significantly higher than the supply. The target percentage of 30% is also a national standard.

239698.explanation: The Labour Party/Green Left believes that people living in poverty should be supported as much as possible. Raising the income threshold helps with this.

239699.explanation: People receiving social assistance benefits should not be obliged but invited to engage in social activities to participate again. Participation works based on trust, not coercion.

239700.explanation: This depends on the extent of the informal care. We believe that a tailored approach is appropriate here.

239702.explanation: The main point is that people in poverty know how to find help and make use of it. Besides the food bank, we would also like to see a "supermarket card" to allow people to buy groceries themselves, thus preventing shame.

239703.explanation: This often concerns people who are difficult to place on the labor market. Additionally, this raises the question of what we want to achieve with deductions: the benefit recipient will probably face (greater) financial problems.

239706.explanation: Public transport is the responsibility of the province. The municipality can encourage initiatives in the villages regarding, for example, shared cars and other small-scale provisions (Burgummobyl).

239707.explanation: The policy in our municipality is to keep the rural area as dark as possible. It has also been shown that more lighting in rural areas can lead to a false sense of security.

239708.explanation: The municipality can stimulate and support initiatives for a shared car. But this should not be an obligation for the municipality. The need for a shared car varies per village.

239709.explanation: Agreed, but we wonder what effect this has. Charging a bike takes a long time, and most people go out with fully charged batteries plus a reserve.

239710.explanation: The Labour Party/Green Left believes there must be a meeting place in every village. We prefer cultural facilities to be combined with other functions.

239711.explanation: Purchasing is not done by the municipality but by the partnership. The Labour Party/Green Left believes the best price-quality ratio should be sought.

239712.explanation: The Labour Party/Green Left believes that social work should be reintroduced in our schools. The deployment of the district team and Kearn has already proven effective. Youth also demand more social guidance at school.

239714.explanation: The right support starts with professional deployment. Care from volunteers and informal caregivers can be a nice addition.

239715.explanation: We find low-threshold care important; however, experience has shown that a counter function per village does not work. In every village, there are multiple low-threshold "counters" available. A good example of this is the nurse practitioners at our general practitioners.

239716.explanation: Our municipality has outsourced this to welfare organizations. Of course, the municipality may also deploy civil servants for this if necessary.

239717.explanation: Cutting back on external consultants should not be an end in itself. It is also important to have some flexibility in the employee base. And occasionally, it is necessary to bring in specialist expertise.

239659.explanation: There is the Villages Budget for this purpose.

239661.explanation: There are better ways to stimulate and promote youth participation.

239662.explanation: The current amount of €3,000 per year in Tytsjerksteradiel is very limited, especially given the importance our social policy attaches to inclusion and equal opportunities.

239663.explanation: Keep it as low as possible.

239664.explanation: Expansion is important, according to need and scale.

239666.explanation: The municipality does not automatically support every sustainable idea from residents, but we are happy to facilitate good plans through our energy desk, where a team is available Monday to Friday from 9:00 to 17:30.

239667.explanation: Civil servants are needed to ensure good service. Hiring external staff should be limited to the strictly necessary.

239669.explanation: Further expansion at this location is not desirable.

239670.explanation: The municipality is not an entrepreneur but can facilitate good plans for renewable energy.

239671.explanation: The municipality is not a bank.

239672.explanation: We stand for a proportional distribution per target group.

239673.explanation: The current support for integration in Tytsjerksteradiel is sufficient.

239674.explanation: Our residents, associations, and villages can organize this well themselves.

239676.explanation: Maintenance of trees and plants is a municipal task.

239677.explanation: The municipality must ensure high-quality and regular maintenance of public greenery.

239678.explanation: Public fishing spots must be accessible to everyone, including people with disabilities.

239682.explanation: Not immediately a financial contribution, but certainly thinking along in solutions.

239684.explanation: Trynwâlden is responsible for this themselves.

239685.explanation: Sports and physical education are important, but funding for this should not come solely from the municipality.

239686.explanation: Multilingual education is beneficial for development.

239687.explanation: Instead of more neighborhood enforcement officers (BOAs), more police deployment is needed.

239688.explanation: Any initiative that contributes to better visibility of the community police officer is desirable.

239692.explanation: The municipality focuses on prevention and avoiding excesses.

239694.explanation: There are currently too few social rental homes.

239698.explanation: People living in poverty need extra support.

239699.explanation: People should not be forced to work but should be encouraged to participate in society.

239702.explanation: The municipality should not provide structural funding but can support on an incidental basis.

239703.explanation: Cutbacks are not desirable. Encouraging and guiding active job seeking is preferred, with enforcement where necessary.

239706.explanation: The municipality should not take over public transport.

239707.explanation: Where this is necessary for safety.

239708.explanation: We want to focus on shared cars, neighborhood buses, and flexible transport: young people can drive, older people can ride along. We also want low-income residents to qualify for free bus and train travel via the Participation Pass.

239709.explanation: This encourages cycling and is good for tourism.

239710.explanation: This is important for livability in every village.

239711.explanation: Quality should not come at the expense of a low price.

239712.explanation: We support having a school social worker.

239714.explanation: Professionals where needed, informal caregivers where possible.

239715.explanation: This is essential for livability in our villages.

239716.explanation: There are other initiatives for this.

239717.explanation: Use own expertise as much as possible.

239659.explanation: The CDA strongly supports grassroots initiatives. Plans must be broadly supported within the community.

239661.explanation: Give young people a structural voice (e.g., youth council) and involve them in policies concerning the village and school. This builds engagement for the future.

239662.explanation: Everyone should be able to be themselves and feel safe. The CDA is strongly against discrimination and insecurity and supports targeted interventions where necessary. Do what is needed. No more. No less.

239663.explanation: We do not want to unnecessarily burden our entrepreneurs. If we raise rates, it must be linked to clear goals (e.g., livability/services).

239664.explanation: The CDA supports expanding sustainable and circular new business parks that fit the wishes of our entrepreneurs in current market conditions.

239666.explanation: The CDA strongly supports cooperative sustainable energy generation and wants to support good initiatives in this regard, considering the new energy law.

239667.explanation: Our civil servants are here to serve our citizens. They do this with passion and dedication. The CDA will continue working on a solid and lean organization in the coming council period and believes in investing to do the right things for our citizens, for example in housing.

239669.explanation: The CDA is not opposed to expansion of Sonac in principle. Expansion is only possible within existing laws and regulations and if livability, health, and nature are sufficiently protected. Concerns of local residents about issues such as odor, traffic, and health must be demonstrably considered in the permit process and lead to clear requirements and enforcement by the municipality or province.

239670.explanation: The CDA supports sustainable and local energy generation, preferably on a cooperative basis and with returns for the local community. Where necessary, the municipality facilitates and participates, especially in the heat transition.

239671.explanation: The CDA sees this as a good instrument to help starters find their first home in our municipality.

239672.explanation: Every municipality is obliged to accommodate a number of status holders. Given the current housing shortage, the priority for status holders could be better balanced against other groups who also have priority for shelter. Status holders should not have priority over other vulnerable groups such as single parents and homeless people.

239673.explanation: By offering newcomers, for example, a membership to a (sports) club, we promote their integration into our villages. This also has preventive effects.

239674.explanation: The CDA believes that integration must not become a task placed on the community. Voluntary initiatives are welcome, but integration policy belongs to the government and professionals, not to village associations or volunteers.

239676.explanation: We want to facilitate villages and neighborhoods with good initiatives, for example, green corridors.

239677.explanation: Our citizens rightly demand better maintenance. The CDA also advocates for better communication regarding mowing policies towards our citizens.

239678.explanation: The CDA supports making a number of fishing spots (not all) wheelchair accessible.

239682.explanation: Sports clubs are extremely important for livability in our villages and neighborhoods and contribute to positive health.

239684.explanation: Based on its Christian values, the CDA supports Christian education where possible and desirable.

239685.explanation: In a healthy Tytsjerksteradiel, people are active. You can't start early enough. The CDA supports alternative physical education classes, for example outdoors.

239686.explanation: The CDA is proud of our Frisian language but does not consider it the municipality's task to facilitate trilingual education.

239687.explanation: Street insecurity is increasing - unfortunately also in our municipality. The CDA is happy to invest in visible enforcement in all our villages.

239688.explanation: Visible neighborhood officers and enforcement officers help reduce nuisance and strengthen the feeling of safety. Safety is a prerequisite for a strong community.

239692.explanation: Due to the alcohol law (NIX18), some of the problems with alcohol and drug use have shifted to private properties. Trust where possible, enforce where necessary.

239693.explanation: The CDA wants to offer every home seeker in our municipality a place within its borders, preferably giving priority to local residents where possible.

239694.explanation: There must be enough affordable housing for starters, families, and elderly people, appropriate to the nature and scale of each village.

239698.explanation: Poverty is a major problem in our society. The CDA finds it important to support people in poverty with facilities and resources through tailored solutions.

239699.explanation: Participation in society and purpose through work are important values for the CDA. However, people with health problems should be exempted.

239700.explanation: Informal caregivers are of great value to our society.

239702.explanation: The food bank operates on volunteers, which the CDA is proud of. Where necessary, the municipality must facilitate a food bank. The CDA advocates cooperation with local farmers and village gardens.

239703.explanation: Benefits are a social safety net that we are proud of. Abuse of this should not be rewarded.

239706.explanation: We consider this per village/location and opt for tailored solutions, affordability, and support. A shared car could optionally be a solution. The CDA advocates doing this in co-creation with the province.

239707.explanation: The CDA supports the current lighting policy (darkness in rural areas) but not at the expense of the safety of our residents. The CDA focuses on so-called smart street lamps.

239708.explanation: Mobility is important to the CDA, but we do not support a general subsidy scheme for a shared car in every village. Mobility needs differ per village. The CDA therefore chooses tailored solutions and coherence with public transport, where shared mobility initiatives can be supported if they match local needs and existing public transport facilities.

239709.explanation: There is a lot of cycling in our municipality, both by residents and tourists. Facilities for this are necessary.

239710.explanation: The village hall or community house is the beating heart of every village. Every village deserves its own shared living room.

239711.explanation: The CDA advocates a good balance between price and quality.

239712.explanation: The tasks of the area team in schools (GIS) and youth workers (Kearn) may, in the CDA's view, be expanded and made more accessible.

239714.explanation: Where possible and desirable, it is fine for care to be taken over by volunteers and informal caregivers, for example through a care cooperative in the village.

239715.explanation: The CDA supports a social desk in each village where care and support are offered through area teams.

239716.explanation: If there is a need for administrative support, the municipality can assess per case whether such support adds value.

239717.explanation: The CDA is not against external advisors if projects are temporary or specific expertise needs to be hired temporarily. But we want to keep the basics in order and mainly invest in knowledge and skills in-house.

239659.explanation: Good plans can always be submitted to the municipality, but we cannot automatically finance them. The council must be able to weigh the plans across all villages.

239661.explanation: The municipal council currently consists of 23 council members plus over 10 citizen members, some of whom are between 18 and 30 years old. To involve young people in politics, projects at schools work much better. That way, we stay close to the youth.

239662.explanation: Everyone belongs, and discrimination is prohibited. This is also stated in the constitution. The municipality does not need to spend money on extra policy. This policy has already cost €105,000: money spent on nice words on paper.

239663.explanation: We should be happy with tourists and recreational visitors in our municipality. They contribute to the local economy. The tourist tax is relatively low and should remain so.

239664.explanation: Currently, there are no commercial plots for sale in our municipality. In our view, every village should facilitate business plots appropriate to its nature and scale.

239666.explanation: The national government offers SDE++ subsidies. There is no need for an additional municipal scheme.

239667.explanation: The VVD wants to focus on core tasks. This can be done with fewer civil servants. The money can be better spent on facilities for our residents and reducing tax burdens for residents and entrepreneurs.

239669.explanation: Sonac fulfills a good role in our society. Extra odor nuisance from new activities is unacceptable for many residents. The VVD wants to work with the national government to achieve further odor reduction.

239670.explanation: The municipality does not need to act as an entrepreneur. It is irresponsible to use public money for risky investments like Solar Park Skûlenboarch.

239671.explanation: The starter’s loan is intended for starters who just can't borrow enough from the bank. The municipality can cooperate with the experienced Housing Stimulus Fund for Dutch Municipalities (SVn) and thus bears little financial risk.

239672.explanation: This is not a good idea. Local residents have an equal right to social housing.

239673.explanation: Integration (participation in society) is largely a personal responsibility. The municipality already has a lot of funding available for various integration programs. Additional funding is not necessary.

239674.explanation: The municipality is grateful for volunteers who want to help people integrate.

239676.explanation: The municipality cannot do everything. To keep public green spaces in good condition, the help of our residents is desired.

239677.explanation: There are many complaints about the state of public green spaces. In 2025, significant extra funds were already allocated for improvements. If possible, perhaps even more funding should be made available for maintenance.

239678.explanation: The VVD faction has so far received no signals from residents that there is a need for this. If there is, the municipality can provide tailored solutions at specific locations.

239682.explanation: The municipality is not Santa Claus. Sports clubs must manage their finances responsibly. In the case of a one-time setback, the municipality can of course provide a one-off extra subsidy, but in the long term, sports clubs must be financially self-sufficient.

239684.explanation: Good education in Trynwâlden is more important than strictly Christian education.

239685.explanation: It is currently well arranged. No extra funding for additional lessons.

239686.explanation: Good idea. Children from Tytsjerksteradiel advance further in the world with a good knowledge of languages. In principle, education can finance this itself, but if they need a one-time subsidy (for example, for the purchase of English math books), we are open to it.

239687.explanation: Tytsjerksteradiel is a quiet municipality. With the current number of BOAs (law enforcement officers), much is already addressed. No additional enforcement personnel are needed.

239688.explanation: Low-threshold contact and accessibility are very good. In some villages, it may be sufficient if residents have the phone number of the local police officer.

239692.explanation: If there are no complaints about nuisance from 'drinking chains' on private property, extra control is not necessary.

239694.explanation: Although the VVD prefers home ownership, there is a need for rental housing. This percentage may increase slightly if there is sufficient demand. Tailored solutions per village.

239698.explanation: There is a category of 'working poor' in the Netherlands. They do not always qualify for support; people on welfare do. Provide these people with support for example in applying for jobs or improving their finances.

239699.explanation: Everyone can do something. There is sufficient work in healthcare or, for example, green maintenance where help is needed.

239700.explanation: Caregiving ranges from light to heavy care. Sometimes someone can still make a useful contribution to the labor market as well. Sometimes not. The situation should be reviewed on an individual basis.

239702.explanation: The food bank has managed well for years thanks to the dedicated efforts of volunteers, sponsors, and local businesses. The municipality does not need to take this over.

239703.explanation: No one benefits from being on welfare for years. The municipality helps people in every possible way towards work. If someone genuinely refuses to cooperate, this must be enforced through a financial incentive.

239706.explanation: No signals indicate a need for this. Residents manage well with the existing offerings: WMO taxi transportation at a low cost, volunteers (e.g., Burgum Mobyl, ANWB automaatje) or a considerate neighbor who drives you. The municipality does not need to establish anything new.

239707.explanation: The municipality's policy has always been to keep the rural area dark. However, society increasingly values safety and visibility.

239708.explanation: The evaluation of the sustainability agenda showed that a shared car did not meet a need among our residents. The projects in Garyp and Hurdegaryp have since been discontinued.

239709.explanation: We have no signals that cyclists experience charging problems. Apparently, shopkeepers or caterers offer sufficient charging or people charge at home. The municipality can better spend its money elsewhere.

239710.explanation: A place to meet is nice, but this can also be in a village pub, for example. We will not impose this as a municipality on villages. It must also be feasible in terms of volunteers and finances. Tailored solutions.

239711.explanation: The municipality spends millions of euros annually on youth care. Negotiating well for a reasonable price for these programs is therefore essential. But price is not the only thing: good care must also be provided.

239712.explanation: There is sufficient provision at our schools, also from the municipal area teams. Additionally, parents have a responsibility to promote their children's well-being. The municipality does not need to solve everything.

239714.explanation: Volunteers and informal caregivers can relieve care, but we will not force residents into informal care such as cleaning for a family member. Professional support must be possible.

239715.explanation: Accessibility of care is very important. Residents should have easy access to care in all villages, for example through their general practitioner or by having a municipal area team worker visit for a kitchen table conversation. The human touch and no counters.

239716.explanation: The municipality is already responsible for the integration of newcomers. Volunteer work can be part of an integration trajectory.

239717.explanation: The municipality has enough policy officers. They know the local situation and can now also make clever use of AI. Expensive external consultants and reports with difficult words are not always necessary.

239659.explanation: Residents know their neighborhood best and often know what is needed to improve livability, safety, and community spirit. If people come with a feasible plan, it is logical that the municipality financially supports them. This can be done, for example, through the village budget.

239661.explanation: We fully agree that young people must be actively involved in local politics. That is the most important thing; the exact form is secondary for us and we would like to discuss this.

239662.explanation: We have ensured that a broad, inclusive policy is created: not for one target group, but all groups that may face exclusion. Whether you have a different cultural background, a chronic disability, or it concerns gender, religion, or sexual orientation.

239663.explanation: As ChristenUnie, we want to make Tytsjerksteradiel more attractive to tourists and focus on more visitors. Increasing the tourist tax is not the most logical lever to pull right now. Our finances do not require this at the moment.

239664.explanation: To maintain livability in Tytsjerksteradiel, space is also needed for entrepreneurship and employment. There is a shortage of good business locations in the region. By carefully enabling new (or better utilized) business parks, local companies can grow.

239666.explanation: If residents jointly generate sustainable energy, as ChristenUnie, we find it important that the municipality supports this; for example through local energy cooperatives, with help and guarantees where necessary. This way the community benefits, it becomes fairer, and our energy system more independent.

239667.explanation: The honest truth is that tasks for the municipality are increasing. Fewer civil servants then means less execution and poorer service. We prefer to save on rules: cut/simplify and make processes smarter.

239669.explanation: The honest truth: the municipality cannot independently “block” this. Anyone promising that makes empty promises. Moreover, expansions can actually reduce odors, noise, and emissions as has happened over the years. Completely locking down Sonac is not a solution.

239670.explanation: We want to achieve our energy goals and want a more local, independent energy system. Therefore, we support local energy cooperatives and invest in solar and wind power, with genuine participation and benefits for the community.

239671.explanation: A starter loan up to €45,000 helps with the purchase of a first home. We mainly target starters from Tytsjerksteradiel or those who want to move back after study/work. This keeps young residents in the municipality and thus strengthens livability.

239672.explanation: We take responsibility for status holders: small-scale, with good guidance and fast integration. But within equal rules: no automatic priority as national legislation has changed. In Tytsjerksteradiel, a maximum of 30% of social housing may go to urgent cases, for all target groups.

239673.explanation: We invest in language and guidance so newcomers quickly participate and go to work where possible. We start early with a broad intake and connect language school, employer, and neighborhood. Real integration only succeeds when you are a full member of the community and can participate.

239674.explanation: We all want to participate. Integration works best in contact with the neighborhood, volunteers, and associations. The municipality encourages this and supports initiatives so newcomers learn the language faster, participate, and feel at home.

239676.explanation: We support neighborhood initiatives that want to maintain greenery in their own neighborhood (Right to Challenge).

239677.explanation: We mow smartly: where necessary for safety and grass use, we mow more often, but in other places less to strengthen biodiversity. This way we keep it neat and green. Here too applies the right of initiative / Right to Challenge by residents.

239678.explanation: Public space must be accessible to everyone. Therefore, we improve accessibility and facilities for people with disabilities, with good paths, safe places, and enough resting points. This way everyone can participate fully and enjoy. This also applies to public fishing spots.

239682.explanation: Sports clubs are the glue of our community. When they face difficulties, the municipality helps where needed, and financially where possible. So that sport remains accessible to everyone and clubs can continue to operate.

239684.explanation: Freedom of education: parents must be able to choose. A Christian school in the Trynwâlden therefore remains important. A clear identity provides direction; the Inspection sees that schools with a clear vision also work better on quality.

239685.explanation: In conversations we have had with schools, the problem is not a lack of money, but a shortage of qualified physical education teachers. As a municipality, we encourage sports at school through initiatives such as High Five for sports and the sports agreement. Additional funding is only provided in case of specific shortages, targeted and temporary.

239686.explanation: Language development is important and we value multilingual education. However, financing this is primarily the responsibility of schools and the national government. The municipality supports where there is local added value or where there are disadvantages but does not provide structural extra subsidies. Individual projects are possible.

239687.explanation: Safety requires visibility on the street.

239688.explanation: Questions are always welcome. What we as ChristenUnie especially want is that neighborhood police officers are visible and easily accessible to residents. Important aspects are low-threshold contact moments and reporting points (physical/digital). It is up to the police to organize this according to needs and capacity in practice.

239692.explanation: We primarily focus on prevention: public order officers and youth work come by in an accessible way to start conversations and make agreements. Only when necessary will stricter enforcement follow.

239693.explanation: We want a fair distribution and optimal turnover. Local ties can count, but can only serve as one of the conditions for urgency, not the sole one. About 30% of the housing is for people with urgent need; the rest is assigned regularly. Building remains the key.

239694.explanation: For ChristenUnie, affordable housing for everyone is important. Therefore, we aim for at least 30% social rental housing in total new construction (currently the total social rental housing is only 19%). This percentage can vary per plan, as long as it collectively moves toward 30%.

239698.explanation: Although Tytsjerksteradiel has relatively less poverty than the average in Fryslân, there are still hundreds of residents who cannot make ends meet or are at risk. It is therefore good to selectively raise the income threshold for assistance so that more people are supported before problems worsen.

239699.explanation: As ChristenUnie, we find participation important: those who can, participate according to their abilities through volunteer work or a learning-work trajectory, among others. But working is not possible for everyone; 30% of people with benefits cannot work due to health problems. Therefore, we choose tailored solutions and no obligation.

239700.explanation: Informal caregivers deserve our appreciation: it is loving care for a loved one and keeps care and community running. Therefore, in cases of intensive informal care, we provide space and tailored solutions: no obligation to apply for jobs, but active support, respite care, good coordination, and fewer barriers when applying.

239702.explanation: No one should suffer from hunger. The food bank in Burgum is an independent foundation, but the municipality can support, for example through subsidies or assistance with housing or other possibilities. This way, we help without penalizing charity and keep assistance close for those who need it.

239703.explanation: Anyone who can work but does not make an effort may have their benefits reduced. But as ChristenUnie, we always first advocate for guidance, making and keeping agreements, and suitable trajectories. Cuts often increase poverty and debts, which ultimately also harms the community.

239706.explanation: Tailored transport between villages is important. A call bus must be quick and easy to book, with a maximum wait time of one hour. Mobility is not just public transport. It can also be through shared cars and initiatives such as Burgum Mobyl, so that everyone remains reachable and can participate.

239707.explanation: Safety is important, but we also want to protect darkness and nature. Therefore, lighting is only provided on unsafe sections and busy routes, preferably smart and dimmable. Where possible, we also improve the paths and markings during maintenance, renovation, or construction of new routes.

239708.explanation: Shared cars make our residents more mobile and accessibility more affordable, especially for those who travel little, have no private car, or are unsure if a second car is necessary. The municipality can provide a start-up subsidy to enable a shared car in every village, together with local initiatives and cooperatives.

239709.explanation: More public charging points for e-bikes in villages help people travel safely and sustainably. We place them at logical locations such as village halls, schools, shops, and sports facilities.

239710.explanation: As ChristenUnie, we invest in the community. Every village deserves a place for meeting. This must be appropriate for the village and its scale: in consultation with residents, cleverly linked to existing buildings and activities (such as school, sports, church, or multi-functional center). This way we strengthen the community without unnecessary new construction.

239711.explanation: As ChristenUnie, we believe quality and continuity for children and youth come first, not the lowest price. We choose appropriate help close by, with good cooperation and as few changes as possible. Costs count, but never as the main criterion.

239712.explanation: We invest in the resilience and mental health of our youth. More social work in schools helps identify problems early and prevents more intensive youth care. Together with youth workers and, for example, neighborhood or sports coaches, we ensure help is close and accessible.

239714.explanation: As ChristenUnie, we believe we should not expect informal caregivers and volunteers to take over professional care. They are indispensable but have limits. For vulnerable residents, professional help must remain available, with good support and respite care for informal caregivers.

239715.explanation: The honest truth is: not every village can have all youth care and Social Support Act (Wmo) care. But we do ensure help is close and humane, with local teams and consultation hours nearby, easily accessible (also digitally and with transport).

239716.explanation: Integration works best through the community: through volunteers, associations, schools, and churches themselves. The municipality can coordinate and remove barriers, possibly in consultation with the COA, but it primarily starts with residents and organizations themselves. It should not become a bureaucratic task.

239717.explanation: We limit the hiring of external advisors and build up knowledge in-house as much as possible. The honest truth is: due to the choice to continue as an independent municipality and with increasing tasks from the national government and requests from the council, hiring is sometimes necessary. Targeted, temporary, and transparent.

239659.explanation: Residents know well what their neighborhood needs. By giving villages budgets and space, we strengthen initiative, ownership, and cooperation. Fewer rules and more trust ensure that good ideas are implemented and no one is left out. We collaborate on how it can be done!

239661.explanation: Young people deserve a voice in their own future. In our municipalities, we do this far too little, which creates distance. By actively involving them, we invest in democratic awareness, relationships, and policies that also work for future generations. Freedom begins with being heard.

239662.explanation: Freedom means that everyone can be themselves. D66 stands for equal rights and protection of minorities. The government must ensure that no one is excluded or unsafe. We are not necessarily for spending less, but we do prefer spending that contributes to this.

239663.explanation: Tourism is valuable but must not come at the expense of livability. By having visitors contribute financially, we invest responsibly in facilities and prevent residents from bearing additional costs. We find it important to visibly invest specifically for tourism entrepreneurs.

239664.explanation: D66 is not in favor of more business parks as a standard solution. We first choose to better utilize existing space and collaborate with entrepreneurs. Growth is possible but only if truly needed, carefully integrated, and does not put pressure on the living environment.

239666.explanation: Local initiatives often show that it is possible. By supporting these, we invest in sustainability, keep control local, and strengthen cooperation among residents. Our attitude is: willing to think along and provide space. It is not just about money but also about thinking along.

239667.explanation: An accessible municipality requires sufficient capacity and knowledge. Fewer rules and better service are only possible if the organization is strong enough to help residents well, but by abolishing rules and certain enforcement, we believe we can achieve more.

239669.explanation: Spatially, expansion can be appropriate if it is carefully substantiated. Health and living environment must be strictly monitored through permits. It is possible but only responsibly and transparently. This must be measurable so that we make the right decisions based on facts and safeguard health.

239670.explanation: Sustainability is necessary for current and future generations. By investing wisely now, we prevent bigger problems later and keep energy affordable and reliable.

239671.explanation: Starters get stuck in the housing market. With a starter loan, we give them perspective and prevent young residents from having to leave. Of course, under conditions and without too much risk, but if possible, we want to invest.

239672.explanation: D66 stands for a fair distribution of scarce housing. Integration requires good guidance and support, but not automatic priority over other housing seekers.

239673.explanation: D66 stands for a fair distribution of scarce housing. Integration requires good guidance and support, but not automatic priority over other housing seekers. Whether this should again be solved with money is the question; more cooperation with partners is at least equally important.

239674.explanation: Good integration helps people participate and become independent. By investing in this, we prevent exclusion and build together a society in which no one is left behind. By seeking cooperation with these parties, we can help each other, including newcomers.

239676.explanation: Residents who want to maintain green spaces themselves deserve that space. This increases involvement, reduces bureaucracy, and creates a more pleasant living environment. It also reduces the gap with our residents. However, this requires accessibility and an open attitude towards cooperation on our part.

239677.explanation: Smart mowing management helps biodiversity and fits sustainable management. More mowing is not automatically better for nature or livability.

239678.explanation: Public spaces must be accessible to everyone. People with disabilities must also be able to recreate and relax. That is freedom in practice, but also a contribution to tourism and recreation and facilitating beautiful places – also for residents without a disability.

239682.explanation: Sports clubs are crucial for health and connection. When they are under pressure, D66 wants to collaborate and invest to keep them sustainable for the future. We do not write blank checks and engage in conversations about efficient deployment and investment. We make arrangements more accessible.

239684.explanation: Freedom of education means freedom of choice, not obligation. The municipality must allow room for diversity and collaborate on quality without fixing one direction. However, conversations and willingness to cooperate and compromise are sometimes necessary; this is tailored work.

239685.explanation: Physical activity is crucial for health and development. D66 invests together with schools and clubs in more physical education. Prevention pays off and prevents later healthcare costs. What you learn at a young age you carry into adulthood, and we invest in that.

239686.explanation: Frisian, Dutch, and English strengthen language skills, identity, and opportunities. D66 invests in this where responsible, together with schools and parents. This has proven valuable for children's development.

239687.explanation: Safety cannot be achieved by enforcement alone. Prevention, trust, and cooperation with residents often work better than extra repression and rules. More help and connection are important. We want to invest in that, but in a targeted and effective way.

239688.explanation: Safety requires tailored approaches per village. D66 wants to work with police and residents to see what is really needed, not impose fixed obligations. A fixed consultation hour is inflexible; we prefer to invest in more accessibility and personal contact, but in different ways.

239692.explanation: More controls do not automatically solve the problem. D66 focuses on awareness, cooperation, and smart solutions. Less punishment, more shared responsibility. This requires a different approach; otherwise, it only shifts the problem.

239693.explanation: Considering local ties helps maintain support. D66 chooses a fair and humane consideration, without excluding others.

239694.explanation: The housing shortage requires clear choices. By investing in social rental housing, we ensure that people with lower incomes can continue to live here.

239698.explanation: More and more people just miss existing arrangements. By responsibly raising the income threshold, we ensure help reaches those who need it. This prevents hidden poverty and makes sure no one falls through the cracks.

239699.explanation: Helping people forward requires trust, guidance, and cooperation. Extra coercion and obligations often backfire.

239700.explanation: Informal care deserves appreciation and support, but full exemption is not always the best solution. D66 opts for tailored support: jointly assessing what someone can manage, with flexibility and guidance, so that care and personal development remain balanced.

239702.explanation: Our motto is 'let everyone be free but no one fall'; we strengthen initiatives and work together, but not necessarily with money. Emergency aid is important, but structural poverty reduction has priority. D66 prefers to invest in prevention and long-term security of existence.

239703.explanation: Sanctions achieve little. Personal guidance and investing in people better help towards work and self-reliance.

239706.explanation: Good transportation is essential to participate. D66 wants to invest in tailored transport between villages, together with residents. If effective and affordable, it increases freedom, accessibility, and prevents people from becoming isolated.

239707.explanation: Good lighting enhances safety and encourages cycling as a sustainable mode of transport. Investment where necessary, responsibly and future-oriented.

239708.explanation: Car sharing offers freedom without everyone needing their own car. D66 encourages this together with villages as a sustainable solution.

239709.explanation: Electric bicycles are becoming increasingly important for sustainable and accessible transport. By targeted investment in additional charging points, especially in villages, we stimulate cycling, increase accessibility, and make sustainable choices feasible for more residents.

239710.explanation: Not every village has the same needs. D66 chooses tailored approaches and collaboration: sometimes sharing or cooperating works better than organizing everything separately.

239711.explanation: Good youth care depends on quality and appropriate help, not the lowest price. D66 wants to steer together with partners based on results, continuity, and expertise. Investing where needed prevents children from needing heavier and more expensive care later.

239712.explanation: Early detection and support at school prevent problems from becoming bigger. D66 prefers to invest upfront, together with education and care, so children and families get timely help and do not fall through the cracks.

239714.explanation: Professional support is indispensable for complex care needs. D66 wants clear agreements: deploying volunteers additionally where responsible and valuable. Where clearly and responsibly possible, support can sometimes be taken over by volunteers. Professionalism and quality/legislation come first. This is transparent, fair, responsible, and protects both clients and volunteers.

239715.explanation: Care close to home increases accessibility and trust. D66 wants to invest together with care partners in support per village so help is quickly, easily, and suitably available for everyone who needs it.

239716.explanation: Volunteering arises from involvement, not obligation. D66 chooses to support and connect, not to direct via civil servants. Collaborating with organizations and residents works better and leaves room for personal initiative.

239717.explanation: D66 is critical of external hiring but does not exclude it. Sometimes specialist knowledge is needed to make good decisions. We choose per situation: invest where necessary and responsible, save where possible.

239659.explanation: BFTD believes that neighborhoods stimulate villages. However, the financial basis must be carefully examined in the implementation.

239661.explanation: BFTD believes local politics should represent every voice of the community, including that of young people. Therefore, according to BFTD, there is no need to establish a youth council.

239662.explanation: BFTD believes everyone is welcome in the municipality of Tytsjerksteradiel and that we should not label people within our municipality. For this reason, no funds are needed for this policy.

239663.explanation: Recently, VAT on tourism and recreation more than doubled. Further tax increases are incomprehensible to BFTD.

239664.explanation: BFTD wants to create room for business activity, especially around the Prinses Margriet Canal. This is an economic lifeline with unique opportunities for port-related companies.

239666.explanation: This is not a core task of the municipality.

239667.explanation: The municipality should focus on its core tasks. Budget cuts may apply to other tasks.

239669.explanation: BFTD believes Sonac is already large enough at its current size. No further expansion is needed at this location.

239670.explanation: This is not a core task of the municipality.

239671.explanation: Starters make the municipality livable and future-proof. The starter loan can be the final push to help this group obtain housing in the already tight market.

239672.explanation: BFTD believes that status holders should not have priority for social rental housing. People from our own municipality should have priority.

239673.explanation: This is not a core task of the municipality.

239674.explanation: As a municipality, we should not burden the people who make our villages livable with issues like integrating newcomers.

239676.explanation: BFTD thinks that if people arrange the maintenance of greenery themselves, costs will decrease and quality will increase.

239677.explanation: Public greenery is the business card of our municipality and should be well maintained. Therefore, BFTD believes it can be mowed more often to maintain quality.

239678.explanation: Fishing is one of the most beautiful sports there is and everyone should have access to it, including people with disabilities.

239682.explanation: BFTD does not see sports and culture as a cost item, but as an investment in vitality and connection.

239684.explanation: BFTD wants good education in the villages. This can be both Christian and public.

239685.explanation: Sports are an important part of a child's development.

239686.explanation: Language aids in a child's development. Also, English is playing an increasingly larger role in the world. Therefore, BFTD believes some investment should be made in education.

239687.explanation: BOAs (officials) cannot guarantee safety. This is the responsibility of the police, and they must handle it.

239688.explanation: The neighborhood police officer serves as the spokesperson of the residents to the police. By organizing a weekly consultation hour, criminal activity can be detected earlier and people feel safer. Safety is worth spending on, according to BFTD.

239692.explanation: The municipality must prioritize combating drug dealing. According to BFTD, this is priority number one, rather than increasing controls at heavy drinking places.

239693.explanation: In Tytsjerksteradiel, too many people are searching for a home but cannot find one because they are not given priority. BFTD believes that our own residents should be given priority because they have a connection to our villages and have often already built a life here.

239694.explanation: By increasing the number of social rental homes in our municipality, more young people get the chance to live independently earlier.

239698.explanation: Simply raising the amount is not the solution in BFTD's view. Tailored solutions work better. Then, the focus is on the individual, not on a group.

239699.explanation: Working stimulates social contacts and contributes to the community (mienskip). Everyone who can work should do so.

239700.explanation: BFTD believes that everyone receiving benefits should continue to apply for jobs.

239702.explanation: Due to the failing national government policy on poverty prevention, municipalities are forced to play a role for their residents. That is why BFTD supports this statement.

239703.explanation: Working stimulates social contacts and contributes to the community (mienskip). Everyone who can work should do so.

239706.explanation: According to BFTD, everyone should be mobile. We are exploring smart combinations between public transport, student transport, patient transport, and Jobinder to combat this form of poverty.

239707.explanation: Better lighting of bicycle paths increases safety. There are still too many unlit bike paths, which can lead to unsafe situations.

239708.explanation: Subsidizing such initiatives is not a core task of the municipality, according to BFTD.

239709.explanation: Cycling is good for health and is a hobby many of our elderly like to do. By facilitating these charging options, elderly can go out more and it contributes to the livability of the villages.

239710.explanation: Village centers promote livability and create bonds in the village. There are also nice combinations possible with village festivals or other local events. BFTD supports village centers but wants to carefully consider the financial basis and consequences when planning.

239711.explanation: According to BFTD, the focus should be on quality and outcomes, not price. Cheap often ends up being expensive.

239712.explanation: More and more young people are dealing with problems that often already show early at school. By investing more in prevention at schools, we prevent children from getting into more trouble. Therefore, BFTD believes more money should be spent on social work at schools.

239714.explanation: BFTD believes it must be possible for informal caregivers to replace professional support. However, it must be ensured that the quality of care does not decrease.

239715.explanation: By offering help locally, we reduce the gap between government and residents.

239716.explanation: Not a core task of the municipality.

239717.explanation: BFTD believes we should have quality in our own town hall and not outsource it.

statements.title: The municipality should provide additional subsidies for trilingual education in primary schools, in Frisian, Dutch and English.

titleParts.glossary: Money the government gives to citizens, businesses, and organizations to support them.

titleParts.text: geven voor drietalig onderwijs op basisscholen, in het Fries, Nederlands en Engels.

statements.title: Care and support must be offered in every village, such as youth care and assistance under the Social Support Act (Wmo).

titleParts.text: In elk dorp moeten zorg en ondersteuning worden aangeboden, zoals jeugdhulp en hulp via de

titleParts.glossary: Social Support Act. The Wmo gives citizens the right to help and support so they can live independently at home as long as possible.

statements.title: The municipality should establish a youth council.

titleParts.glossary: A group of young people who provide input and discuss plans and policy with the municipality.

statements.title: Residents who have a plan to improve their neighborhood should receive funding from the municipality for that.
The municipality should spend less money on policies for LGBTIQ+ people.

titleParts.text: De gemeente moet minder geld uitgeven aan

titleParts.glossary: A plan for how a problem will be addressed.
LHBTI stands for lesbian women (L), gay men (H), bisexuals (B), transgender people (T) and intersex persons (I).

statements.moreInfo: The municipality has a policy to ensure that everyone can participate in society. This is called the Broad Inclusion Agenda. This policy is for various groups of people, for example people with disabilities, people with low incomes, elderly people, young people and people who feel different because of their religion, origin, sexual preference or gender. Tytsjerksteradiel is also a Rainbow Municipality. That means the municipality pays attention to the safety and acceptance of people who are lesbian, gay, bi, transgender or intersex (LGBTIQ+). For this the municipality reserves €3,000 each year.

statements.title: There must remain a Christian school in the Trynwâlden.
Residents who want to generate sustainable energy together should receive financial support from the municipality.

titleParts.glossary: Energy based on resources that are effectively infinite, such as sunlight and wind.

titleParts.text: op willen wekken, moeten financiële steun krijgen van de gemeente.

statements.title: The municipality should reduce spending on civil servants.

titleParts.glossary: Spending less money.

statements.title: Municipal enforcement officers (BOAs) should more often check groups engaging in heavy public drinking.

titleParts.glossary: Special Enforcement Officers. They can issue fines and often supervise the streets.
A place, often in a shed or hut, where young people gather to drink alcohol.

statements.title: People receiving social assistance should, in return, do socially useful work.

titleParts.glossary: Money from the municipality for people who have no income or personal funds.
For example cleaning the streets or keeping company and supporting elderly residents.

statements.title: People buying a house for the first time should be able to take out a municipal starter loan of up to €45,000.

titleParts.text: Mensen die voor het eerst een huis willen kopen, moeten via de gemeente een

titleParts.glossary: An extra loan on top of your mortgage, which you can only get when buying a house for the first time.

statements.title: The municipality should prevent the expansion of the company Sonac in Sumar.
statements.moreInfo: Sonac is a company in Sumar that processes animal by-products and residual streams. The company has long been located in Sumar. Sonac wants to expand in the future. The municipality and the province must decide on that. There has been much debate about this earlier in the municipal council.

statements.title: At least 30% of the newly built homes in Tytsjerksteradiel must be social rental housing. Currently, 19% of homes in Tytsjerksteradiel are social rental housing.

titleParts.text: Van de nieuw te bouwen woningen in Tytsjerksteradiel moet minimaal 30% een

titleParts.glossary: A rental home with a low rent (in 2026 at most €932.93 per month) for people with a low income.

titleParts.text: zijn. In Tytsjerksteradiel is nu 19% van de woningen een

titleParts.glossary: A rental home with a low rent (in 2026 at most €932.93 per month) for people with a low income.

statements.title: The municipality should spend more money on integrating newcomers into society.

titleParts.text: De gemeente moet meer geld uitgeven aan

titleParts.glossary: Newcomers learn the language and customs of the Netherlands.
People who are new to the Netherlands.

statements.title: The municipality should mow public grass more often.
If sports clubs are struggling, the municipality should contribute financially.

titleParts.text: Als sportverenigingen het moeilijk hebben, moet de gemeente

titleParts.glossary: Something related to money.

statements.title: All villages in the municipality should have their own social and cultural village center.
The municipality should invest money in a food bank.

titleParts.text: De gemeente moet geld steken in een

titleParts.glossary: An organization that provides free food to people with low incomes.

statements.title: Tytsjerksteradiel should have more municipal enforcement officers (BOAs) who focus on safety.

titleParts.glossary: People who, for the municipality, check whether citizens and businesses comply with the rules.

titleParts.text: (boa’s) komen die zich met veiligheid bezighouden.

statements.title: The municipality should invest in tailored transport between villages (for example, a dial-a-ride bus).

titleParts.text: De gemeente moet geld steken in

titleParts.glossary: A type of public transport that doesn't run on a fixed schedule but can be called to pick you up.
A bus that only runs if you reserve it in advance. The bus then takes you door to door.

statements.title: The municipality should give primary schools extra funding for gym classes.
Cycle paths in rural areas should be better lit.

titleParts.glossary: Area within the municipality's borders but outside the towns or villages themselves; there are few houses and other buildings here.

statements.title: The tourist tax should be increased.

titleParts.glossary: The tax you must pay when you stay overnight in a hotel or at a campsite.

statements.title: The municipality should subsidize making a shared car available in every village.

titleParts.glossary: Money the government gives to citizens, businesses, and organizations to support them.

titleParts.text: geven om in elk dorp een deelauto mogelijk te maken.

statements.title: People receiving welfare benefits who provide informal care to a family member or acquaintance do not have to apply for jobs.

titleParts.glossary: Money from the municipality for people who have no income or personal funds.

titleParts.text: die een familielid of bekende verzorgen als

titleParts.glossary: Someone who provides unpaid and often long-term care for sick family members or friends.

statements.title: The municipality should deploy civil servants to help newcomers get involved in volunteer work.

titleParts.text: De gemeente moet ambtenaren inzetten om

titleParts.glossary: People who are new to the Netherlands.

statements.title: When allocating social housing, applicants from the municipality should be given priority.

titleParts.glossary: A rental home with a low rent (in 2026 at most €932.93 per month) for people with a low income.

titleParts.text: moeten woningzoekenden uit de eigen gemeente voorrang krijgen.

statements.moreInfo: Social rental homes are allocated by the housing corporation. National and municipal rules apply. People with urgent problems always get a home first. The municipality may then take people from its own municipality into account. In Tytsjerksteradiel this is already done as much as the law allows.

statements.title: Professional support must not be replaced by volunteers and informal caregivers.

titleParts.glossary: Assistance from trained, paid professionals, such as caregivers or support workers.

titleParts.text: mag niet vervangen worden door vrijwilligers en

titleParts.glossary: Someone who provides unpaid and often long-term care for sick family members or friends.

statements.title: The municipality should not spend money on renewable energy projects, such as solar panels and wind turbines.

titleParts.text: De gemeente moet geen geld uitgeven aan projecten voor

titleParts.glossary: Energy based on resources that are effectively limitless, such as sunlight and wind.
These are modern wind turbines that convert wind power into electricity and can be up to 150 meters high.

statements.title: The municipality should more often reduce welfare benefits if people are not actively seeking work.

titleParts.glossary: To give less money or to reduce a monetary amount.
Money from the municipality for people who have no income or personal funds.

titleParts.text: als mensen niet actief naar werk zoeken.

shootoutStatements.title: The municipality should make funds available for residents who want to maintain the trees and plants in their neighbourhood themselves.
The municipality should cut back on external consultants.
The municipality should make public fishing spots more accessible for people with disabilities.
More business parks should be developed in Tytsjerksteradiel.
The municipality should provide more public charging points for electric bicycles in villages.
The municipality should support more people in poverty by raising the income threshold for assistance.
The municipality, together with the partnership, should focus primarily on price when selecting organisations for youth care.
The municipality should invest more money in social work at schools.
The municipality must ask the police to hold a weekly meeting with the community police officer in each village.
The municipality must continue to give refugees with a residence permit priority for social housing.
The municipality should ask volunteers, associations and village initiatives to help integrate newcomers.
